source_title: Modern Investigative Journalism: A Comprehensive Curriculum Based on the ARIJ Method
author: Mark Lee Hunter, Luuk Sengers, Marcus Lindemann

extracts.extract: investigative reporting requires skills and attitudes that most reporters never learn. Yet most reporters can indeed succeed at investigative work, if they acquire the appropriate methods and the desire to use them.
extracts.notes: From John Ullman's Reporter's Handbook argument
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core investigative identity and methods-based approach to journalism is independent of any technology era.

extracts.extract: the various processes of investigative work – the conception, planning, researching, organization and composition of a story – were thought of as separate tasks which must be completed in a sequence of discrete steps. In 2009, the publication of Story-Based Inquiry demonstrated how they could be fused into a single continuous workstream. The benefits include economies of time – an explicit reply to the notion that investigative work is always slow and costly – as well as increased quality of information and storytelling.
extracts.chapter: Teaching economic and social aspects of investigative journalism
extracts.notes: Key claim about integrated vs sequential workflow
extracts.relevance_rationale: Story-based inquiry workflow concept endures but LLMs enable even faster integration of research/writing steps.

extracts.extract: What the public wants from us is not a catalogue of information, but the meaning of the information that we have catalogued. That meaning is captured in a story. In other words, investigative reporting is not merely about finding information. It is about telling a story based on new and important information.
extracts.relevance_rationale: The principle that journalism is about meaning-making through narrative, not just cataloguing facts, is timeless.

extracts.extract: That is the key difference between facts and stories: We forget facts alone, but we remember facts that are embedded in a story
extracts.relevance_rationale: Cognitive principle about stories vs isolated facts is a permanent feature of human cognition.

extracts.extract: Conventional journalism rarely involves stories that involve more than a few days to research and write. Investigations can last for weeks or months. They also involve far more information than is generated by research for most news stories. Thus a higher level of organization is required. The organizational work becomes ever more important in direct proportion to the amount of material that the reporter collects.
extracts.notes: Research: Going wide and deep subsection
extracts.relevance_rationale: Information management in long investigations endures but LLMs transform how reporters organize and retrieve large volumes of material.

extracts.extract: authentic investigative reporters do not accept something as true simply because someone, and in particular an official, gave them a report. Instead, they verify the information through other sources, and challenge information that cannot be verified. Nor do they consider someone more important because he has an official title. The importance of a source to an investigative reporter is based on the validity of the information that the source can provide.
extracts.notes: Source relations: from dependence to challenge subsection
extracts.relevance_rationale: Independent verification and challenging official claims is a permanent investigative principle regardless of tools.

extracts.extract: The investigator's job is to arrive at a judgment – that something should never have happened, that it must never happen again, or conversely, that something worthwhile has been unjustly terminated.
extracts.notes: Outcomes: Proving the need for change subsection
extracts.relevance_rationale: The investigative judgment function — arriving at evidence-based conclusions about wrongdoing — is inherently human.

extracts.extract: The most important question here is whether or not we exclude facts that conflict with our goals. An investigator takes all the facts that can be found into account. Though some facts are more meaningful than others, they cannot be eliminated simply because we don't like them.
extracts.notes: On practical objectivity vs ideological objectivity
extracts.relevance_rationale: Intellectual honesty about not excluding inconvenient facts is a timeless epistemic principle.

extracts.extract: the reporter earns the right and the confidence to make a judgment about the meaning of the facts, through an investigation.
extracts.notes: Neutral toward facts, but earns right to judge their meaning
extracts.relevance_rationale: Earning the right to judgment through rigorous investigation is a fundamental journalistic standard.

extracts.extract: The reporters collected internal documents of the Nixon campaign, including an organisational phone directory. The phone directory contained names and titles, and was thus a physical map of the organisation.
extracts.chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
extracts.notes: Woodward and Bernstein technique
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using organizational documents to map power structures remains essential investigative technique.

extracts.extract: The reporters targeted people at lower managerial levels. Executives would lie to them or track them. Janitors would not see as much as hoped and have heard less. The people in the middle saw and heard a great deal and could do little to affect it.
extracts.chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
extracts.notes: Woodward and Bernstein middle-management targeting
extracts.relevance_rationale: Targeting mid-level organizational insiders as sources is a timeless human intelligence technique.

extracts.extract: To keep track of these documents and of their interviews, they developed an elaborate filing system, modeled on the cross-referencing system used by the FBI. In this system, whenever a source mentioned events involving another source, copies of both their interviews were placed in each other's files.
extracts.chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
extracts.notes: Woodward/Bernstein FBI-modeled filing technique
extracts.relevance_rationale: Cross-referencing filing systems remain essential but LLMs and knowledge graphs can now automate the cross-linking.

extracts.extract: In their meetings with sources, they paid attention not only to information, but to emotion. In particular, they noticed that many of their sources were afraid. This, beyond the facts they initially collected, alerted them to the importance of the story.
extracts.chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
extracts.relevance_rationale: Reading emotional cues from sources to gauge story significance is an irreplaceable human skill.

extracts.extract: Since Watergate, professional practice has been to use information from anonymous sources only when four of them agree on the same facts.
extracts.chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
extracts.notes: Four-source agreement rule post-Watergate
extracts.relevance_rationale: Multi-source corroboration threshold for anonymous sources is a permanent verification standard.

extracts.extract: No one source is key to an investigation, and it is a fatal mistake to think that a 'deep throat' will tell you everything you need to know.
extracts.chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
extracts.relevance_rationale: Source diversity principle and avoiding single-source dependency transcends technology eras.

extracts.extract: If a source really knows a secret, he or she has few reasons to share it with you, and the most common is to manipulate you. After all, if the information is secret, and you trust the source, the odds are good that you will publish the information without further verification.
extracts.chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
extracts.relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward source motivations and manipulation risk is a permanent epistemic safeguard.

extracts.extract: No reporter can prevail alone. There must be other actors, in the media and in society, who are willing to act on the information that the reporter provides.
extracts.chapter: The Image and reality of the Investigative Journalist
extracts.relevance_rationale: Coalition-building for investigative impact is about social dynamics, not technology.

extracts.extract: Davies suggests that investigative reports tend to begin with one of several categories of information. We refer to them as i) 'tips', ii) 'leftovers', or iii) observations and questions.
extracts.notes: Nick Davies taxonomy
extracts.relevance_rationale: Story origin categories (tips, leftovers, observations) describe permanent patterns in how investigations begin.

extracts.extract: News media are full of leftovers – little stories that seem to shout, 'There's more to find here\!' but are usually left to rot. If you read your own newspaper you'll see all sorts of leads in the story that the original reporter just didn't have time to pursue. Remember that these leads will often appear as anomalies – facts that just seem strange, or out of place.
extracts.notes: Walt Bogdanich quoted
extracts.relevance_rationale: Mining news leftovers for story leads endures but LLMs can now systematically scan and flag anomalies across large news archives.

extracts.extract: We will notice something that seems strange, and we will imagine what might explain the mystery. Then we will see how we can verify our idea.
extracts.notes: Core investigative cycle in miniature
extracts.relevance_rationale: The observe-hypothesize-verify cycle is the core investigative logic regardless of tools used.

extracts.extract: This is the principle: If something in the environment has suddenly changed, or appears to be out of place, there may be a story behind it.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Anomaly detection as story trigger is a timeless investigative instinct.

extracts.extract: There are always more stories available for investigation than we can ever finish. So the reporter must assess the time and resources that are necessary to the task, and measure them against the intrinsic value of the story.
extracts.notes: Deborah Nelson story-evaluation framework follows
extracts.relevance_rationale: Resource assessment and story prioritization are permanent editorial judgment tasks.

extracts.extract: It is worth noting that Allain is not looking to blame people: Her target is the system, not the individuals who can only fail to cope with an impossible situation. This is a classic dilemma of investigative reporting: Do we attack individuals, or seek to change the system that enables their corruption?
extracts.relevance_rationale: Systemic vs individual framing dilemma is a permanent editorial and ethical question.

extracts.extract: She learned how to create and administer surveys, and to interpret the results.
extracts.notes: Allain investigation skill acquisition
extracts.relevance_rationale: Survey design and administration endures as a skill but LLMs can now assist with survey creation and analysis at scale.

extracts.extract: She learned how to solicit sensitive information from individuals engaged in unethical or illegal activity. She did so, in essence, by asking officials if victims were telling the truth when they said that they were forced to pay bribes. Instead of accusing them, she asked, 'Why do you need this money?'
extracts.notes: Indirect questioning technique from Allain
extracts.relevance_rationale: Non-accusatory questioning to elicit sensitive information is a permanent interpersonal interviewing technique.

extracts.extract: Before undertaking an investigation, ask yourself if you want to become expert on the milieu where the story takes place. If students do not care about legal issues, they will never find the energy to investigate the justice system.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Intrinsic motivation and domain interest as prerequisites for sustained investigation are timeless.

extracts.extract: The most basic of those methods is the one that you are now going to teach your students: The use of hypotheses to frame and guide investigative research. We cannot investigate efficiently unless we know what we are investigating, and what we are looking for. Otherwise we will either pile up information until we drown in it, or wander around helplessly with no idea of where to find the information we need. Therefore, we need an idea of what a story might be, before we can investigate whether or not it really occurred that way, or in another way.
extracts.notes: Core method of GIJN since 2003
extracts.relevance_rationale: Hypothesis-driven investigation as core method to avoid information drowning is more relevant than ever with LLM-era data abundance.

extracts.extract: as investigative journalists we create a hypothesis – a story that we have reason to believe might be true - and then set out to verify that story. As we proceed, we will discover either that our initial idea was more or less accurate, or we will uncover evidence that points to another story.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Iterative hypothesis testing and refinement is a permanent investigative logic.

extracts.extract: it is very, very hard to investigate a story by compiling an enormous number of facts, and then trying to make sense of them. The usual result is drowning in a chaotic sea of details. In order to investigate efficiently, we need a provisional idea of what we are investigating.
extracts.notes: Against inductive-only fact gathering
extracts.relevance_rationale: Warning against inductive-only fact gathering is even more critical when LLMs can generate vast information.

extracts.extract: A hypothesis is not mere speculation. We do not invent facts to explain what we cannot see. We create a theory that accounts for what we already know and what we can see. Then we deduce the facts that might explain what we do not know, and seek to verify them.
extracts.notes: Standards for making hypotheses
extracts.relevance_rationale: Distinguishing hypothesis from speculation via evidence-based deduction is a permanent epistemic standard.

extracts.extract: The hypothesis must contain assertions (or terms) that can be verified. No one of any intelligence volunteers information to a reporter simply because the reporter asks for it. However, many sources will confirm or deny information that is already in the reporter's possession. By creating a hypothesis, we see clearly what information we must acquire before we speak with anyone. These facts will make us more powerful when we proceed to interviews.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Verifiability as prerequisite for hypothesis terms, and using documents to gain leverage in interviews, is timeless.

extracts.extract: In its initial form, the hypothesis must be written in three sentences or less. A story combines facts into a sequence of events that leads to a meaning. It can, and must, be easily summarised. Otherwise your students do not understand what they are trying to prove.
extracts.notes: Three-sentence rule for hypothesis
extracts.relevance_rationale: Three-sentence hypothesis constraint for clarity is a permanent pedagogical and investigative discipline.

extracts.extract: Encourage them also to start every answer with a human subject, followed by an active verb. We find this a useful trick to provide concrete answers - and concreteness is what we need, if we will ever be able to prove the story.
extracts.notes: Human subject + active verb = provable claim
extracts.relevance_rationale: Human subject plus active verb for concrete, provable claims is a permanent writing and investigation technique.

extracts.extract: Encourage the students to start every question with 'How...' We think it is better to focus students on how things occur, than on why they occur. The 'how' points to concrete, verifiable actions, while the 'why' ends with unverifiable motives.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Preferring how over why questions for verifiability is an enduring investigative framing principle.

extracts.extract: every investigation becomes more difficult as it proceeds; if they cannot do the easy work first, they will never be able to do the hard work.
extracts.notes: Easiest verification points first
extracts.relevance_rationale: Easy-first verification sequencing is a permanent research strategy principle.

extracts.extract: Making a hypothesis seems time consuming. But with practice, it can be done in minutes. The time lost when you have no hypothesis is measured in days, weeks and months. The larger point is that news reporters are trained to do everything fast, and that is their default mode. Investigators must be less reactive, and more pro-active; they must take control of the story from the start.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Hypothesis as time-saving device and proactive investigation stance is timeless methodology.

extracts.extract: An honest reporter constantly tests his or her hypothesis against the facts that emerge in the investigation.
extracts.notes: Guard against hypothesis blinding
extracts.relevance_rationale: Continuously testing hypothesis against emerging facts to guard against confirmation bias is permanent.

extracts.extract: After the hypothesis, the timeline is the most important tool used by investigators, and probably the most widely used. It helps us to make sense of complex matters, by revealing causality and consequences of related events. It shows us what is missing from our story—that is, what might have happened before and after events that we have confirmed – and keeps the information we have gathered in a clear, strong order. A timeline also creates a foundation for the simplest and strongest narrative structures. In fact, the timeline enables us to begin creating our narrative while we are still conducting research.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Timeline as core analytical tool endures but LLMs can now auto-generate and maintain timelines from document ingestion.

extracts.extract: They can easily identify the separate events by counting the verbs in the hypothesis: every verb is the expression of a different action.
extracts.notes: Practical technique for breaking hypothesis into events
extracts.relevance_rationale: Decomposing hypothesis into discrete events via verb counting is a permanent analytical technique.

extracts.extract: It helps to begin every sentence with a human subject. It is easier to investigate human beings than abstract problems or systems. Moreover, at some point, students will need human sources to verify the events. In short, students should ask not 'What do I want to investigate?' but 'Who do I want to investigate?'
extracts.notes: Formulate events concretely subsection
extracts.relevance_rationale: Framing events around human subjects for investigative concreteness is timeless.

extracts.extract: Make sure that the character in the event is engaged in an activity. Only activity leaves traces that we can follow up to prove that something happened. 'Landlords cheat' is less easy to prove than 'Landlords demand exorbitant rents and large security deposits.' 'Demanding' is a real action (the landlord is talking to the student), while 'cheating' is an abstract summary, and a subjective opinion at that.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Insisting on concrete, verifiable actions over abstract characterizations is a permanent investigative standard.

extracts.extract: urge your students to stay clear of negatives and predictions. 'Landlords never return the money to students' states what does not happen. Things that don't happen are hard to prove, so let's change the sentence to state what does happen instead: 'Landlords refuse to return the money to the students.'
extracts.notes: Avoid negatives and predictions in timeline events
extracts.relevance_rationale: Framing assertions positively for verifiability is a timeless writing and investigation discipline.

extracts.extract: The hypothesis should be framed to reveal the most important story we can find here, without merely speculating. We need to know what the most important story may be, so that we can decide whether or not it is worth exploring. If the most important story we can hypothesize from the available facts is neither very interesting nor very easy, we abandon it.
extracts.notes: Identify maximum and minimum story subsection
extracts.relevance_rationale: Maximum vs minimum story evaluation for go/no-go decisions is a permanent editorial judgment.

extracts.extract: one of the common, and gravest, errors made by investigative reporters is to make accusations that their evidence cannot fully support, in order to make the story appear more dramatic.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Warning against overreach beyond evidence is a timeless credibility standard.

extracts.extract: Journalists realized that instead of simply recording what official sources told them, they could conceive and document a story on their own. The focus of their work shifted from gaining access to powerful figures to confronting powerful figures with facts. Reporters were thereby forced to become expert at finding and documenting facts before, not after, they asked questions.
extracts.notes: Post-Watergate methodological shift
extracts.relevance_rationale: Shift from access journalism to document-based confrontation with facts is a permanent methodological advance.

extracts.extract: Donald L. Bartlett and James B. Steele urged other journalists to develop 'a document state of mind' — to always think of what documents might be available to support their stories.
extracts.notes: Bartlett and Steele at Philadelphia Inquirer
extracts.relevance_rationale: Document state of mind is a permanent investigative mindset regardless of whether documents are physical or digital.

extracts.extract: Ordinary reporters ask their sources, 'What happened?' Investigative reporters can also ask their sources: 'Isn't this what happened?' The second question is much more powerful than the first. It shows the source that the reporter cannot easily be lied to. It also makes the source's task, of answering the question, much easier. Sources do not like to explain everything to reporters. Nor do they like to volunteer information that a reporter clearly does not possess. However, sources often confirm information that they would not volunteer.
extracts.notes: From Lina Joudy Syria archaeology case
extracts.relevance_rationale: Confirmation-based questioning technique is a permanent interviewing skill for investigative reporters.

extracts.extract: the reporter's documents clearly inform and guide her use of human sources. The documents are connected to institutions where her sources work. By knowing their contents, the reporter can engage in detailed and precise discussions with experts and officials.
extracts.notes: Documents as bridge to human sources
extracts.relevance_rationale: Documents informing and guiding use of human sources is a timeless investigative workflow principle.

extracts.extract: It is a misunderstanding that journalism is about finding facts; instead, it is about finding reliable sources. Once you find the right documentary and human sources, the facts will emerge from them. However, hunting for sources consumes the largest share of a typical investigation. It is fair to say that 90% of journalists use only 10% of the available sources, precisely because finding new sources takes time.
extracts.notes: 90/10 source usage estimate
extracts.relevance_rationale: Finding reliable sources remains essential but LLMs dramatically reduce search costs and can surface sources journalists would otherwise miss.

extracts.extract: For investigators, it is usually far more efficient and fruitful to seek information from public documents first. The information they contain can confirm whether or not a story is plausible, before much more time is invested in it.
extracts.notes: Putting documents first subsection
extracts.relevance_rationale: Documents-first strategy for plausibility testing before investing time remains a sound investigative principle.

extracts.extract: The fact that something is open to inspection does not mean that anyone inspects it. Often, publicly available documents contain answers to important questions, but no one has bothered to study the documents.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Publicly available documents hiding in plain sight is a permanent investigative observation.

extracts.extract: Open sources, in particular documentary sources, can be consulted without raising an alarm. Calling an official for an interview will attract attention, and doors may close.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Operational security advantage of consulting open sources before alerting targets remains essential.

extracts.extract: Even if someone is willing to speak with you, he or she will provide better information if you are visibly capable of understanding it. The source will observe whether or not you understand the language and concerns of the people involved in your story. You must therefore do at least some documentary research in order to prepare for interviews.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Documentary preparation to demonstrate competence to sources is a permanent interpersonal dynamic.

extracts.extract: First we identify the people who are concerned by our story, and the open sources that may be related to them. Then we plan a route among these sources. If we know whom we expect or hope to meet, we have a better chance of being prepared when an opportunity appears. If we have a strategy to reach them, our chances of making contact increase.
extracts.notes: How to search for open sources subsection
extracts.relevance_rationale: Systematic source mapping and route planning is a permanent investigative planning technique.

extracts.extract: Which of these characters will create a documentary record of his or her actions? Which of them will use a document to justify his or her actions?
extracts.notes: Using timeline to identify open sources subsection
extracts.relevance_rationale: Predicting which actors create documentary records is a timeless source-identification technique.

extracts.extract: Wadih Al Absi's answer was 'to go back to the law, and look for gaps in the law.' No one could stop him from reading Yemen's laws.
extracts.notes: When official sources refuse to speak, laws are always accessible
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using legal texts as always-accessible sources when officials refuse to speak is a permanent investigative fallback.

extracts.extract: We can imagine whom these other characters may be by creating a 'source village'. Along with actors in the story, the 'village' contains various witnesses to events or their consequences. Besides helping us to think of new sources, a source village can help us to visualize the sources we already know, and their relations to each other. This is particularly useful when investigating business or criminal networks.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Source village concept for network visualization endures but LLMs and graph tools can now automate network mapping from documents.

extracts.extract: journalists who focus on inside sources at the start of an investigation leave themselves with few options in the event that their targets refuse to speak. By identifying numerous opportunities to contact people, we ensure that at least some information will flow to us.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Source redundancy and diversification as risk mitigation is a permanent investigative strategy.

extracts.extract: reporters must take great care not to become the toy of organisations or individuals that offer access. Sources who control information may decide to show only the parts that advance their interests. Thus once the reporter has entered the village, and learned useful information, he or she must proceed to others.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Warning against source capture and access bias is a timeless journalistic independence principle.

extracts.extract: We begin our interviews with the people who are most open to us.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Starting with most accessible sources is a permanent interview sequencing principle.

extracts.extract: The core strategy here is to put sources before information. Students can be trained, instead of looking for an answer on the Internet, to look for reliable sources, and then to look for the answer in these sources. The Internet is not a source. It is a container of sources, like a library.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Sources before information and treating the internet as container of sources, not a source itself, is even more relevant with LLMs.

extracts.extract: Students should be advised that 'search costs' - the time and effort it takes to find the solution to a given problem - are a main reason that investigation is regarded by editors and publishers as slow and costly. By improving their efficiency, they make investigative reporting a viable enterprise.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Search cost reduction remains key for investigation viability but LLMs have fundamentally changed the economics of information retrieval.

extracts.extract: Journalists have a dreadful habit of relying on secondary sources for scholarly and scientific articles - that is, a newspaper story about a discovery - instead of the primary source. Investigators need the primary sources, and they are not hard to find in specialised databases and search engines.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Insistence on primary over secondary scholarly sources is a timeless research quality standard.

extracts.extract: rather than try to discern the ideal or normative ethical position in a given situation, reporters may find it simpler and more effective to consider the risks involved. What dangers may arise, for the reporter and for others, from a given course of action? How can the reporter manage, reduce or eliminate those risks?
extracts.notes: Risk-based ethical framework rather than normative
extracts.relevance_rationale: Risk-based ethical framework for investigative decisions is a permanent practical ethics approach.

extracts.extract: Investigative reporters must never be merely the servants of a cause. They must first seek to discover the truth of a given situation. Positive change cannot be founded on illusions, no matter how noble they may seem.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Independence from causes and commitment to truth-seeking is a permanent investigative principle.

extracts.extract: in investigative work, as in all reporting, anyone who is implicated in a story as an actor or target must be given the opportunity to reply to any questions raised by the story, and to explain any facts concerning him or her.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Right of reply for implicated parties is a permanent ethical and legal standard.

extracts.extract: Very frequently, reporters are offered 'scoops' that are essentially disinformation, based on the testimony of one or another parties to a scandal, or on a purportedly 'secret' document. In such cases the information should not be used unless the reporter can verify it independently.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Skepticism toward scoops and requirement for independent verification is timeless and even more critical with AI-generated disinformation.

extracts.extract: Corroboration serves that purpose: What other sources support the claims of the reporter's anonymous sources? They can be found through documenting the source's claims, or by confirming with three other human sources, anonymous or not.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Corroboration through documents or multiple human sources is a permanent verification standard.

extracts.extract: If a journalist is offered such documents, he or she is probably wise to accept them. However, the journalist will be even wiser to keep the documents in a safe place, outside his or her home or office. The journalist must also take care to ask the provider of the documents if he or she can be identified as their likely human source. If so, the journalist must never refer or allude to the documents. The information they contain must be documented by other means.
extracts.notes: Put the source above the secret subsection
extracts.relevance_rationale: Source protection and operational security for confidential documents is timeless and arguably more critical in the digital era.

extracts.extract: It is fine to have an emotional reaction to what you see. But then you must confirm that what you have seen is true, and that your emotions are not misleading you.
extracts.notes: From Allain Divorced Women in Jordan case
extracts.relevance_rationale: Separating emotional reaction from verified facts is a permanent epistemic discipline.

extracts.extract: Never accept confidential information without asking the source how many other people know it, and if he or she can be identified as the source. Treat vulnerable sources as off the record. This means that you cannot use the information, or allude to it, unless you can find another source whom you can identify.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Source protection protocols for confidential information and vulnerable sources are timeless ethical obligations.

extracts.extract: To be legitimate undercover work must meet a double test: Is the story of great public importance, and is there no other way of doing it?
extracts.notes: Double test as ethical threshold for clandestine methods
extracts.relevance_rationale: Double test for undercover work legitimacy (public importance + no alternative) is a permanent ethical threshold.

extracts.extract: The undercover work was supported by extensive documentary research concerning the companies that owned the schools, and by interviews with government officials and parents.
extracts.notes: Zbiss Quranic kindergartens case; undercover as supplement not substitute for open-source work
extracts.relevance_rationale: Triangulating undercover findings with documentary and interview evidence is a permanent verification principle.

extracts.extract: By profession, journalists are opportunists. They grab what they can quickly take, and don't waste time longing for what seems out of reach. This attitude serves well in news reporting, where the reporter is always running after the action. But in investigative reporting, we usually need sources that other reporters don't care or know how to access, in order to verify a story.
extracts.notes: Distinguishes investigative from news reporting by source strategy
extracts.relevance_rationale: Distinguishing investigative from news reporting by source strategy and persistence is a permanent identity distinction.

extracts.extract: One place to search for confidential sources is the story's timeline. Each event in the timeline concerns various participants and witnesses. If they have not been previously cited in news stories, there is a good chance that they have something to say, as well as good reasons for not saying it.
extracts.notes: Timeline as source-discovery tool
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using timeline events to identify uncited sources is a permanent source-discovery technique.

extracts.extract: A reporter who already knows most or all of the facts that can be obtained from open sources will always be of interest to sources, including confidential sources, because the reporter is visibly capable of understanding what the source has to say.
extracts.notes: Reporter expertise as precondition for source access
extracts.relevance_rationale: Reporter expertise as precondition for source trust and access is a permanent interpersonal dynamic.

extracts.extract: He takes a collegial approach to investigating complex subjects, in which the reporter assumes that sources are acting in good faith, and continually builds expertise in their domain... From this reading he collected the names of actors in the story: While reading, you start laying a plan for interviewing. I highlight key people who are either authors or quoted in stories who indicate some level of familiarity with the topic, and write down a list with their names and apparent areas of expertise.
extracts.notes: Schapiro method: building source list from document reading
extracts.relevance_rationale: Building source lists from document reading endures but LLMs can now extract and compile actor names from large document sets automatically.

extracts.extract: I obtained press accreditation to attend trade-fairs of carbon project developers in which people engaged directly in the market gather with their peers – where I could speak with key players in a less formal and threatening fashion. These settings can be invaluable in having off and on the record conversations to deepen your understanding, and setting relationships into motion that can later lead to 'lucky' revelations.
extracts.notes: Schapiro method; 'lucky' in quotes signals revelations are product of preparation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using informal settings like trade fairs for source access and relationship building is a permanent field technique.

extracts.extract: Aggression is not a substitute for research; most often, it is a sign of incompetence, or of fear. Dogs do not show their teeth because they are smart or brave. Before confrontation occurs, a reporter must acquire expert knowledge of the subject, the sources and their activities.
extracts.notes: Research before confrontation; aggression as incompetence
extracts.relevance_rationale: Research before confrontation and aggression as sign of incompetence is a timeless investigative principle.

extracts.extract: In general, people find it easier to confirm information than to volunteer information. We can use our hypothesis for this purpose, especially if the reluctant source is someone we believe to be honest. We can say, for example: 'This is what I think happened. Is that accurate? Please tell me if I am wrong.'
extracts.notes: Hypothesis as interviewing tool to unlock reluctant sources
extracts.relevance_rationale: Using hypothesis to unlock reluctant sources via confirmation rather than volunteering is a permanent interview technique.

extracts.extract: A 'paper trail', in reporters' slang, is a chain of documentation that establishes causality or responsibility for a given event. There are two simple ways to imagine a paper trail that includes documents of interest to us.
extracts.notes: Definition of paper trail concept
extracts.relevance_rationale: Paper trail concept and tracing chains of documentation for causality is a permanent investigative method.

extracts.extract: We can deduce the existence of many documents that might have appeared at a moment in our timeline. For example, a meeting will be followed by a letter or memorandum that sums up participants' conclusions, or a business deal will be sealed by a contract. Many projects start with plans, followed by tender offers, tenders and contracts, then status reports when things go into motion, followed by evaluations.
extracts.notes: Using timeline to predict which documents should exist
extracts.relevance_rationale: Deducing which documents should exist from timeline events is a permanent investigative reasoning technique.

extracts.extract: Most often, confidential documents are provided by human sources. We can predict who, among the actors in our story, is most likely to possess a given document by asking the following questions: Who asked for the document? Who created it? Who approved it? Who would receive a copy? Who shared it? Who responded to it?
extracts.notes: Six questions to identify document holders
extracts.relevance_rationale: Six questions to identify document holders is a permanent source-mapping technique.

extracts.extract: It is easier to find something when you are looking for it. That is why they need to imagine at least some documents in advance – so they can say, for example, 'Did you sign a contract? Do you have a copy? Please show it to me.'
extracts.notes: Anticipating documents makes them findable
extracts.relevance_rationale: Anticipating documents to make them findable is a permanent investigative preparation principle.

extracts.extract: In most investigations, the first sources to speak are those who feel victimised. The second most talkative group, once they see our interest in the story, will usually be expert witnesses who can provide perspective on events. In general, those responsible for a bad situation are the last who wish to speak about it.
extracts.notes: Victim-first, perpetrator-last interview sequence
extracts.relevance_rationale: Victim-first, perpetrator-last interview sequencing is a permanent investigative workflow.

extracts.extract: The ideal source in most investigations is someone in the middle ranks of an organisation, at operational or planning levels. These people have access to significant documents, but have very little influence on how policies are formed or implemented. They may thus consider themselves victims, obliged to carry out policies or actions in which they do not believe.
extracts.notes: Mid-rank sources as ideal: access to documents, motivation to speak
extracts.relevance_rationale: Mid-rank organizational insiders as ideal sources due to access and motivation is a permanent human intelligence principle.

extracts.extract: A general goal is to access the source's unique qualities. These include: insight, observations, experiences, judgments, or documents. When we have collected them, these unique qualities become our assets.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Identifying unique qualities of each source (insight, observations, documents) is a timeless interview planning principle.

extracts.extract: Before we interview someone, we should know, at a minimum, what role they probably played in our story. Are they victims, witnesses, or perpetrators? Which other actors or events may they have observed? We should also know any facts about them that we can collect from the Internet – a CV, interviews, news, etc.
extracts.notes: Backgrounding the interviewee before contact
extracts.relevance_rationale: Backgrounding interviewees endures but LLMs can now automate comprehensive background research from internet sources in minutes.

extracts.extract: Students can structure source conversations around the following types of questions: Key question: if you could ask only one question, what would it be? Objective questions: Is this what happened? Who was there? Where? When? How? Timeline: When did an event start? How? What happened next? Subjective questions: What do you think about the events? What did you feel? Resource questions: Who else will know about this? Whom should I speak with? Did anyone create a report, memorandum or other document about these events?
extracts.notes: Five question types for investigative interviews
extracts.relevance_rationale: Five question types for structured investigative interviews is a permanent interviewing framework.

extracts.extract: The goal of an interview is not to ask questions, but to listen! An investigator encourages people to talk, and observes how they speak – their emotion, body language or silence – as well as what they speak about.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Listening as primary interview goal and reading nonverbal cues is an irreplaceable human investigative skill.

extracts.extract: Before ending an interview, professional reporters do the following: Quickly review their notes to see if anything needs clarifying; Ask: 'Is there anything I did not ask, but you would like to tell?'; Ask: 'Do you feel that you had a chance to tell everything you wanted?'; Ask whom the source believes they should speak with next; Ask for documents in possession of the interviewee.
extracts.notes: Five-step interview closing checklist
extracts.relevance_rationale: Five-step interview closing checklist is a permanent best practice for investigative conversations.

extracts.extract: Journalists should not look at officials as though they are untouchable – they are public servants, there to serve us, not vice versa. Journalists have to be well prepared, knowing exactly why they are interviewing and what they want, and have enough ammunition to show the official they are expert.
extracts.notes: Rana Sabbagh on interviewing powerful sources
extracts.relevance_rationale: Treating officials as public servants and being well-prepared is a timeless power-dynamics principle.

extracts.extract: From the beginning you should be in touch with the target – maybe they will change your story. Maybe you are wrong. You can ask for general information. Or later you will say, 'You told me this, but this evidence says it isn't true. Give me answers.' From the beginning I am in touch, but in different ways. At first I seem naïve: 'Please give me all your information about my topic.' Then when I know I have all the evidence I can be more direct and stronger.
extracts.notes: Sabbagh graduated-pressure approach: naïve first, confrontational later
extracts.relevance_rationale: Graduated-pressure interview approach from naive to confrontational is a permanent interviewing strategy.

extracts.extract: The underlying principle remains the same: creating statistically valid analyses of events and phenomena that could otherwise not be accurately understood.
extracts.chapter: The Masterfile: Creating a database for an investigation
extracts.notes: Core epistemological claim about data journalism from Meyer tradition
extracts.relevance_rationale: Statistical analysis of events and phenomena for investigative purposes is a permanent epistemological principle.

extracts.extract: Data is not limited to numbers. Every time that reporters write down an idea, or interview someone, or save an article that they read on the Internet, in order to process and analyse it later, they are compiling data. The real issue is whether or not they compile data in a useful way. Strictly speaking, data are isolated facts kept or presented in a structure.
extracts.chapter: The Masterfile: Creating a database for an investigation
extracts.notes: Broad definition of data; structure is what makes facts into data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Broad definition of data as structured facts regardless of format is a permanent conceptual foundation.

extracts.extract: It is indisputable that every leading professional in this field organises data as an investigation proceeds, instead of waiting for the investigation to be over before organising. This is the justification for asking your students to learn at least one method.
extracts.chapter: The Masterfile: Creating a database for an investigation
extracts.notes: Universal practice among top investigators: organize as you go
extracts.relevance_rationale: Organizing data progressively during investigation rather than after is a permanent best practice.

extracts.extract: When we create a timeline, we create a chronological structure for our story. We will now make that same structure the backbone of our database. We start with the hypothesis and the timeline... If they know the date at which a given event occurred, they enter that data into the file. The date may include the year, month, day, or even the hour when the event occurred. In other words, it should be as precise as possible. Any date format can be used, but it must be consistent.
extracts.chapter: The Masterfile: Creating a database for an investigation
extracts.notes: Timeline-as-database-backbone method
extracts.relevance_rationale: Timeline-as-database-backbone method endures conceptually but LLM-era tools can auto-structure chronological databases from ingested documents.

extracts.extract: Next, they insert in this new space a full description of a source that gives information about that particular event. This technique enables them to keep their information and its source together... Such sources may be marked in the file with DOCUMENT: to make them easy to search for by using the function Control + F. Whatever term is used, it must be used consistently throughout the file. The terms used should also be terms that do not appear in the dictionary, to avoid false hits.
extracts.chapter: The Masterfile: Creating a database for an investigation
extracts.notes: Consistent markup tags (DOCUMENT:, LOG:, NOTE:, TO DO:) for searchability
extracts.relevance_rationale: Source attribution and tagging for searchability endures but manual DOCUMENT/LOG markup in Word files is displaced by modern knowledge-management and LLM-assisted tools.

extracts.extract: The biggest advantage of putting everything in a single Word-file is that we will actually structure the story while we research. The masterfile merges both processes.
extracts.chapter: The Masterfile: Creating a database for an investigation
extracts.notes: Research and writing as merged process via masterfile
extracts.relevance_rationale: Merging research and writing in a single file endures as principle but the Word-file masterfile approach needs updating for LLM-era collaborative tools.

extracts.extract: Never put anything on your computer that might compromise a confidential source. Confidential human sources should be referred to by code names, and confidential documents (including interview transcripts) can be kept elsewhere – for example, in an online depository, behind a strong password.
extracts.chapter: The Masterfile: Creating a database for an investigation
extracts.notes: Operational security for masterfile: code names, separate storage
extracts.relevance_rationale: Source protection with code names endures but digital security practices need updating for LLM-era threats like AI-powered deanonymization.

extracts.extract: Students must present: An investigative hypothesis; A timeline; A source map; Open sources that can validate parts of the hypothesis and timeline; Documents that the group expects to obtain from human sources.
extracts.notes: Five required components of an investigation proposal
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: I always start with a simple one sentence objective for investigation and then – after scouring all the material written already, after consulting the Internet and databases – write up a simple 'State of Knowledge Memo' that summarises in just a few pages what are the key pieces of information already known, the key 'known unknowns' and how I propose to uncover those.
extracts.notes: Stephen Grey method: one-sentence objective + state of knowledge memo
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Inefficient processes lead to vast amounts of wasted time. In particular, reporters compile massive amounts of information about institutions or individuals without first defining a hypothesis that would enable them to focus on viable stories.
extracts.chapter: Audiences, Values and markets for investigative reporting
extracts.notes: Hypothesis-first as antidote to information overload
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Poor information management creates legal risks. Reporters in a hurry often make grave mistakes, because they have not understood what sources are telling them... few reporters are trained in archiving techniques that could enable them to control and catalogue the information they collected. Consequently, they are only partly aware, or not aware at all, of the gaps in their documentation.
extracts.chapter: Audiences, Values and markets for investigative reporting
extracts.notes: Poor archiving as source of legal and factual risk
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Opportunity costs – that is, the foregone profits from not being able to seize an opportunity – rise when investigations waste resources. Most of the data and documentation that reporters acquire is either never catalogued, or thrown away after a project was completed. Thus valuable information that could be re-packaged for consulting or further publication was discarded.
extracts.chapter: Audiences, Values and markets for investigative reporting
extracts.notes: Data as reusable asset; discarding documentation as waste
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Investigative reporters achieve results most effectively when they form coalitions with other social forces. Put another way, isolated reporters rarely prevail in their search for reform or justice. Other actors must be engaged, both in order to protect the reporter from reprisals, and to carry through a reform agenda.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism as a social force
extracts.notes: Protess et al. coalition model as key mechanism for investigative impact
extracts.relevance_rationale: AI/automation landscape has evolved significantly since this was written

extracts.extract: Some typical means of collaboration include: Sharing sources, such as lists of contacts, or data, or access to witnesses. Though investigative reporters must verify all of the information in their possession before using it, they can also obtain documentation, insights or data from others. Whistleblowers and NGOs are among the most common sources of such material.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism as a social force
extracts.notes: Shared sources still require independent verification
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: It is not enough to be right. A wise investigative journalist will ensure, as well, that he or she is not alone with the truth. In order to reduce this risk, the journalist should ensure: The facts of the story are not overstated or exaggerated... The facts, and a viable path to the facts, must be open to other journalists... Social actors can be engaged, as sources and as potential allies, before the story is published.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism as a social force
extracts.notes: Three conditions for surviving publication: no overstatement, reproducible facts, pre-engaged allies
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Investigative reporting adds most and best to the fortunes of a media enterprise when it is perceived as setting a standard of independence for the entire format... There must be close management of investigations, to ensure that they are well conceived and executed, that budgets are respected... Investigations are not simply 'thrown over the wall'. They are announced in advance, in particular to allies who will support them... Hostile response from targets of the investigation is anticipated and prepared for.
extracts.chapter: Audiences, Values and markets for investigative reporting
extracts.notes: Four operational conditions: branding, professionalism, promotion, defense
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: Turning information into data – that is, giving it a structure and order – prepares the subsequent creation of a narrative order. Certain pieces of information – in particular, great quotes from human or documentary sources – can be transposed directly into the final narrative. Ideas or insights that occur to us as we research a story often turn out to be highly useful as well.
extracts.notes: Masterfile as bridge between research data and narrative
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: We describe two prototypical narrative structures: A chronology, and an odyssey (or picaresque structure). The former is appropriate for stories in which the passage of time is the key organizing factor, such as reconstructions of crimes, biographies or profiles, or stories about how policies go wrong. The picaresque structure is well-suited to stories in which related phenomena occur simultaneously in multiple locations.
extracts.notes: Two prototypical structures for investigative narratives
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Before making any changes in the masterfile, students must save it under another title. All revisions to source material should be saved in this way. We recommend adding a date stamp in the file name, to ensure that a student can find successive versions of a given file.
extracts.notes: Manual version control via date-stamped file copies
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: We urge you to require that students prepare a footnoted version of their stories, in which they provide a reference for every quote and fact. Interviews should be accompanied by the date on which they occurred, as well as contact information for the source (unless the source must remain anonymous). Documents should be referenced with full bibliographic and page information.
extracts.notes: Footnoted version as verification mechanism
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: When information was scarce, most of our efforts were devoted to hunting and gathering. Now that information is abundant, processing is more important. We process at two levels: 1) Analysis to bring sense and structure out of the never-ending flow of data and 2) presentation to get what's important and relevant into the consumer's head.
extracts.notes: Philip Meyer quote; shift from gathering to processing
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Data journalism is a genre of journalism where data drives the story. It is a process through which data is gathered, filtered and creatively visualized, relating a compelling tale in ways that narrative alone may fail to do.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: 'Data' is not a synonym for 'numbers'. Data consists of facts that have been archived and organized, and that can be analyzed to uncover patterns and structures of fact. The facts may consist of numbers, but they may also consist of events, judgments, names, places, or any other category of information. Once they are organized so that they can be queried and interpreted, they become a database.
extracts.notes: Expanded data definition for data journalism context
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Data journalism requires the following four steps: 1-Defining the question 2-Locating the data 3-Cleaning the data 4-Visualizing the story
extracts.notes: Four-step data journalism pipeline
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Data journalism is a hybrid of social science inquiry and traditional journalism. It borrows scientific rigor from the former, and storytelling from the latter. And, as is the case in both fields, you cannot start a data journalism story without a specific question that leads to a hypothesis. Data is often a confusing, sometimes boring set of raw information. If you do not have a starting point, or an angle, through which you will examine and analyze documents or numbers, they will not mean much.
extracts.notes: Hypothesis-first for data journalism; hybrid of social science and journalism
extracts.relevance_rationale: AI/automation landscape has evolved significantly since this was written

extracts.extract: Students may argue that data issued by authoritarian governments is not reliable. Please explain that this may, precisely, be the story. Journalism students and journalists can extract data from a given authoritarian government and compare the numbers with statistics issued by international organizations such as the United Nations or the World Bank, or by NGOs. The gap between the two data sets becomes the story.
extracts.notes: Unreliable official data is itself a finding; gap between data sets is a story
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Search by filetype: Google allows you to filter your search by type of document. Punch in the word you are looking for, then add filetype:xls for Excel or filetype:pdf for PDF... Search specific sites: Let's say you only want to check suicide rates published by the UNDP. Go to Google and type the following: suicide rates by country 2015 filetype:xls site:www.undp.org
extracts.notes: Google advanced search operators for finding structured data
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: Find scattered data from more than one report and compile them yourself to create one comprehensive Excel document... Gather the information yourself where not available. If you cannot find a document comparing uses of Facebook, Twitter and Instagram in your country, create a survey that you will administer yourself. Use crowdsourcing if need be.
extracts.notes: Three fallbacks when no single dataset exists: compile, survey, crowdsource
extracts.relevance_rationale: Manual data processing method can be augmented or streamlined by LLMs

extracts.extract: In some cases, they will not find any data no matter how much they look. This, however, is by itself a finding. The absence of data can, indeed, become the story.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Once data sheets are located, you must make sure you clean your data. This means getting rid of any mistakes, typos or blanks in the sheet, that could compromise your calculations and confuse or even distort your story.
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: A human being most probably entered the data. He or she had to enter hundreds of names, along with date of birth, gender and occupation. Probably exhausted at one point, the government employee did not notice that he or she used the words 'male' and 'man' at the same time. It will be your job to clean the data by making sure only one word refers to the male gender.
extracts.notes: Concrete example of terminology inconsistency requiring cleaning
extracts.relevance_rationale: Data acquisition and cleaning workflows benefit from LLM-era tooling updates

extracts.extract: Once your data are clean, you will have to find patterns in the data that tell a story. You must start, as explained earlier, with a specific and newsworthy question. It also helps to use data visualization, converting, for example, tables to graphs or pie charts. Once the numbers are transformed into graphs, you can identify striking differences or comparable patterns.
extracts.notes: Visualization as analysis tool, not just presentation
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: In some cases, they will not be able to find trustworthy data. In such cases, they must put their findings in context by: Giving the source with a critical comment... Using multiple data sets and comparing sources. For example, they can compare police records listing reported incidents of rape with a NGO's statistics.
extracts.notes: Two strategies for untrustworthy data: critical sourcing and cross-comparison
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Scrape websites to extract data, where data tables are not available. If you have access to a computer programmer, he or she can help you mine data that is unavailable in Excel format online from governmental, non-governmental or corporate databanks.
extracts.notes: Scraping as data acquisition when structured data unavailable
extracts.relevance_rationale: Specific tool workflows change significantly with LLM-assisted alternatives

extracts.extract: Investigative work means getting and verifying information, through one's own efforts, that was not simply provided, unsolicited, by a source. It is not only about revealing secrets. By providing our audience with information that they cannot verify for themselves, we create value for them.
extracts.chapter: Audiences, Values and markets for investigative reporting
extracts.notes: Definition of investigative work as self-initiated verification
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: Investigative reporting does not mean taking a report created by someone else and rewriting it. Like any other source, the partners in a coalition must be regarded by a journalist as liable to error and capable of manipulative tactics. Like any other document, the veracity and authenticity of third party reports must be verified independently.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism as a social force
extracts.notes: Coalition partners still require independent verification
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling

extracts.extract: The story began when a journalist in Australia came into possession of a hard drive containing millions of pages of raw data on offshore bank accounts, involving thousands of individuals across the world. But the data was effectively useless until a team of allied reporters in New Zealand, led by Nicky Hager, figured out how to interpret it. At that point, teams in different countries accepted the task of investigating individuals whose names appeared in the files.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism as a social force
extracts.notes: Offshore Leaks: raw data useless until interpreted; distributed investigation model
extracts.relevance_rationale: Newsroom organization and collaboration patterns remain relevant regardless of AI tools

extracts.extract: In France, for example, there are two tests of whether libel has been committed. The first is whether the information provided by a reporter is 'complete and coherent.' The second is whether or not the reporter acted in good faith, by trying to consider all sides of a story and verify the facts.
extracts.chapter: Investigative journalism as a social force
extracts.notes: French defamation test as verification standard: complete, coherent, good faith
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core journalistic procedure remains essential regardless of tooling

extracts.extract: Numerous important investigations began when a reporter prepared a story on one aspect of what turned out to be a much greater subject. In such cases a common pattern emerges: As the reporter becomes sensitive to a subject, simultaneously, he or she is developing new skills and confidence. In effect, the story trains the reporter.
extracts.notes: Story trains the reporter; start small, grow expertise
extracts.relevance_rationale: Core data journalism insight persists regardless of AI tooling
